summary: Gruber recounts the brief controversy over Apple's developer transition kit (DTK) exchange program for the Apple Silicon transition. Developers paid $500 for DTK hardware with no warranty and clear terms requiring return, but expected a generous exchange like the 2006 Intel transition where $1000 DTKs were swapped for $1300 iMacs. Apple initially offered only $200 credit toward M1 Macs expiring in May, which developers found miserly given the precedent and their contribution to Apple's platform. Within two days, Apple reversed course, offering full $500 credit valid through year-end and usable on any Apple product. Gruber praises the quick correction but questions why Apple didn't have the offer ready months earlier when M1 Macs shipped. He frames it as a lesson in recognizing and fixing mistakes quickly.
key_points: The DTK terms were explicitly no-warranty, no-exchange — developers agreed to get nothing back, making any credit purely goodwill
Apple's initial $200 credit offer was tone-deaf given the 2006 precedent of exchanging $1000 DTKs for $1300 iMacs
Developers contributed to Apple's platform success by porting apps early, making the relationship mutually beneficial rather than one-sided charity
Apple's two-day reversal to $500 credit with extended expiration and broader use was a textbook example of quickly fixing a mistake
The real failure was not having the exchange program ready when M1 Macs launched in November, months before the DTKs needed to be returned
money_quotes: Pretty sweet deal, considering everyone thought they'd have to send back the DTKs in exchange for a steaming pile of corporate gratitude.
A $200 credit for a $500 DTK rental seemed miserly coming from the richest company in the world.
Developers used those DTKs to help Apple as much or more than to help themselves.

The objection to those objections is rather obvious, though: every developer who paid for one of these DTKs agreed to very clear terms that promised nothing at all except, well, an implied steaming pile of corporate gratitude.
Make few mistakes, but recognize the mistakes you do make quickly, admit to them, and fix them. That's the recipe.
My quibble isn't that Apple made a mistake with the amount of the 'appreciation credit', but with why it took them so long to make the initial offer.
Apple should have done whatever it could to get developers to move from DTKs to production M1 Macs as soon as possible.
key_insight: Apple's DTK saga illustrates that the best corporate crisis management isn't avoiding mistakes entirely, but recognizing them fast and making them right — though the real miss was not acting months earlier.
